questions.prompt: Why should TTFT and throughput be diagnosed separately?
options: They're the same metric expressed in different units
TTFT is driven by network + prefill while throughput is decode-driven — different causes, different fixes
Throughput only matters for batch jobs
TTFT is set by the tokenizer
questions.explanation: Time-to-first-token reflects prompt processing (prefill) plus network; tokens-per-second reflects sequential decoding. A slow TTFT wants a smaller prompt or caching; slow total duration wants fewer output tokens or a faster model — and streaming fixes perceived latency either way.

questions.prompt: What does the lesson call the biggest single cost lever?
options: Negotiating enterprise discounts
Model right-sizing — routing and cascades that send each task to the smallest model that passes your evals
Removing all system prompts
Disabling streaming
questions.explanation: Frontier models cost orders of magnitude more than small ones, and many tasks don't need frontier quality. Routing simple traffic to small models — with the eval suite proving quality holds — is the step-change saving; token trimming is the steady accumulation on top.

questions.prompt: Which cost/latency practices does the lesson recommend? (Select all that apply.)
options: Routing non-interactive work to batch APIs for ~50% savings
Setting max_tokens deliberately and instructing concise output
Logging per-request usage and latency as a prerequisite to optimizing
Self-hosting immediately, since it's always cheaper than APIs
questions.explanation: Batch APIs halve costs for anything that can wait; output tokens are the slowest and priciest, so bound them; and you can't optimize what you don't measure. Self-hosting with vLLM wins only at high, steady utilization — at low or spiky volume, the API is cheaper.

questions.prompt: When does self-hosting with vLLM beat paying for an API?
options: Always — GPUs are cheaper than tokens
At high, steady utilization where continuous batching keeps the hardware busy
Only for models above 100B parameters
Whenever latency doesn't matter
questions.explanation: Self-hosting trades per-token cost for fixed GPU cost plus operational burden. Continuous batching makes the economics work only when traffic keeps the hardware utilized; idle GPUs are pure loss, which is why spiky or low-volume workloads belong on APIs.

questions.prompt: What exactly does provider prompt caching reuse?
options: The model's final answer for similar questions
The prefill KV-cache computation for a repeated prompt prefix
The embeddings of previous queries
The network connection between requests
questions.explanation: Prompt caching skips re-computing the internal state (KV cache) for an exact-matching prompt prefix — saving input cost and TTFT, but not generation. That's why it shines for agents and RAG with large stable system prompts and tool definitions.

questions.prompt: How do Anthropic and OpenAI differ in prompt-cache mechanics?
options: Anthropic uses explicit cache_control breakpoints; OpenAI caches long prompt prefixes automatically — both report hits via usage fields
Anthropic caches outputs; OpenAI caches inputs
OpenAI requires a separate caching API; Anthropic doesn't support caching
They're identical in every detail
questions.explanation: With Anthropic you mark cache breakpoints explicitly; OpenAI applies prefix caching automatically on longer prompts. Either way, verify you're actually getting hits by reading the cache-related usage fields — assumed caching is a classic silent cost leak.

questions.prompt: What's the key risk of semantic caching (serving cached responses for similar queries)?
options: It's slower than calling the model
False hits: a confidently served cached answer to a subtly different question — threshold conservatively and audit hits
It only works with one embedding model
Cache storage costs exceed inference costs
questions.explanation: Embedding similarity treats 'cancel my subscription' and 'why was my subscription cancelled?' as neighbors. A false cache hit is a wrong answer served instantly and cheaply. Conservative thresholds, scoped use, and hit auditing are the controls.

questions.prompt: Which statements about layering caches are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: Prompt caching, exact-match response caching, and semantic caching save different costs at different risk levels
Exact-match response caching skips the whole model call but only fires on identical requests
Stable-first prompt ordering is what makes prompt caching effective
Semantic caching is risk-free since embeddings capture exact meaning
questions.explanation: The three layers complement each other: prefix caching cuts prefill cost on every request, exact-match caching eliminates calls for repeats (key on model + prompt + params, with TTLs), and semantic caching raises hit rates at real false-hit risk — it is anything but risk-free.

questions.prompt: Why aren't system-prompt rules sufficient as a safety mechanism?
options: System prompts are too expensive to include
Prose instructions shape behavior but don't enforce it — they fold under adversarial pressure and long-tail inputs, so independent rails must run outside the model
Providers strip safety instructions from system prompts
Rules in prompts slow down generation
questions.explanation: Module 2's lesson holds: prompt rules are suggestions. Production safety comes from layers that run regardless of what the model does — input rails before the call, output rails after, and permission gates around actions.

questions.prompt: Which belong on the input-rail side? (Select all that apply.)
options: Topic/intent classification with a canned redirect for out-of-scope requests
Moderation scoring via purpose-built classifiers (e.g., Llama Guard, moderation APIs)
PII redaction before the model call when the model doesn't need the data
Schema validation of the model's response
questions.explanation: Intent scoping (also a cost control), moderation, and inbound PII redaction all run before the model sees the input. Schema validation is an output rail — it checks what the model produced, alongside content rules and outbound PII scans.

questions.prompt: How should blocking vs flagging be divided?
options: Block everything a rail is unsure about
Block bright lines (PII leaks, policy violations); flag the gray zone for human review while shipping, and alert on flag-rate spikes
Flag everything; never block
Alternate between the two randomly to confuse attackers
questions.explanation: Blocking is for unambiguous violations; flagging ships the response but queues it for review, absorbing the uncertain middle without frustrating users. Flag-rate spikes on a dashboard are an early-warning signal for drift or attack campaigns.

questions.prompt: Why does every blocking guardrail need its own eval set?
options: Regulators require guardrail documentation
A rail that wrongly declines 2% of legitimate requests frustrates those users at every interaction — its precision/recall must be measured and tuned like any classifier
Eval sets make guardrails run faster
Without one, the guardrail can't be deployed
questions.explanation: False positives are a product cost paid continuously. Each blocking rail is a classifier: measure it on labeled cases including benign-but-edgy ones, tune blocking toward precision, and let flagging absorb the recall.

questions.prompt: Why is prompt injection described as structural rather than a patchable bug?
options: Providers refuse to fix it
Everything in the context — instructions and data alike — is just tokens attended by the same mechanism, so the model can't reliably tell whose instructions to follow
It only affects open-weight models
Firewalls can't inspect LLM traffic
questions.explanation: Instruction/data separation doesn't exist at the architecture level: a retrieved document that says 'ignore your instructions' is processed by the same attention as the system prompt. The builder's job is bounding the damage of inevitable bypasses, not 'fixing' injection.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes indirect prompt injection from jailbreaking?
options: Indirect injection requires physical access to servers
Injection hides attacker instructions in content processed on a victim's behalf (webpages, documents, tool results); jailbreaking is a user talking the model out of its own safety training
Jailbreaking is the more dangerous of the two for applications
They're synonyms
questions.explanation: Jailbreaking is user-vs-model; injection is attacker-vs-application, with the victim never seeing the attack arrive via RAG corpus, browsed page, or MCP tool result. Conflating them leads to defending the wrong layer — indirect injection is the application builder's nightmare.

questions.prompt: What are the three legs of the 'lethal trifecta'? (Select all that apply.)
options: Access to private data
Exposure to untrusted content
An exfiltration channel
Use of a fine-tuned model
questions.explanation: When one system combines private-data access, untrusted-content exposure, and a way for data to leave (email, HTTP, even markdown image URLs), hidden instructions can silently exfiltrate with the user's permissions. The design move is breaking at least one leg — fine-tuning is irrelevant to the pattern.

questions.prompt: Which defenses hold even when the model is fully compromised by an injection?
options: Delimiters around untrusted content and injection-screening classifiers
Structural layers: least-privilege tools, human approval gates, egress allowlists, and sandboxes
A strongly worded system prompt
Running at temperature 0
questions.explanation: Textual defenses (delimiters, screeners) reason about content and get bypassed; capability constraints bound what a compromised agent can actually do. Treat an agent reading untrusted content as if that content's author were at the keyboard — and permission it accordingly.

questions.prompt: What is counterfactual bias evaluation?
options: Asking the model whether it is biased
Varying only a sensitive attribute (or proxies like names) across otherwise identical cases and measuring whether outcomes shift
Comparing your model's bias to a competitor's
Removing all demographic data from training sets
questions.explanation: It's a golden-set technique: identical inputs except the name/dialect/location, aggregated over many cases, with alerts when outcome gaps exceed threshold. Run it in CI like any eval — model upgrades can shift bias, not just quality.

questions.prompt: What makes human oversight of high-stakes AI decisions real rather than theatrical?
options: The human sees the model's reasoning and sources, has authority and time to disagree, and their override rate is measured
A human clicks approve on every recommendation as fast as possible
The model explains itself in legal language
Oversight is logged but never reviewed
questions.explanation: A reviewer approving 100% of recommendations at 3 seconds each is a rubber stamp — and regulators increasingly say so. Genuine oversight needs information (reasoning, sources), empowerment, and measurement of whether disagreement actually happens.

questions.prompt: Which privacy practices does the lesson require? (Select all that apply.)
options: Knowing your provider's retention policy and whether API traffic trains models (verify, don't assume)
Redacting PII before model calls where the model doesn't need it
Making deletion requests reach traces AND vector-store embeddings
Assuming enterprise tiers handle privacy automatically
questions.explanation: Privacy is configuration you must actually do: retention windows, zero-data-retention agreements where needed, PII redaction, and deletion that reaches everything — including what's embedded in RAG indexes. Assumptions are how data ends up where it shouldn't.

questions.prompt: How does the EU AI Act determine your obligations as an application builder?
options: By your company's size
By your use case's risk tier — the same API call is minimal-risk in a writing aid and high-risk in a hiring tool
By which model provider you choose
By your server's physical location only
questions.explanation: The Act regulates by risk tier: banned uses, high-risk (employment, credit, essential services — heavy logging/oversight/documentation duties), limited-risk (transparency), minimal-risk (unregulated). Classify your use case early; retrofitting high-risk obligations is expensive.

questions.prompt: What recurring lesson does the capstone identify across all eight modules?
options: Bigger models solve most production problems
The model is the smallest part of the system — quality, cost, safety, and reliability come from the engineering around it
Python is preferable to TypeScript for AI work
Production systems should avoid LLM providers
questions.explanation: A demo needs a model and a prompt; production needs evals, failure handling, cost controls, grounding, guardrails, observability, and compliance. Every module taught a version of this: the durable engineering lives in the harness, not the weights.

questions.prompt: How should checklist items be skipped, when they're skipped?
options: Silently, to keep documentation light
Deliberately and on the record — an internal summarizer needs less than a hiring tool, but the decision should be documented, not forgotten
Never — every item applies to every system
By deleting them from the checklist
questions.explanation: Stakes scale the list: not everything applies everywhere. The failure mode is omission by accident. A recorded 'we skipped X because Y' is auditable and revisitable; a forgotten item is an incident waiting to be discovered.

questions.prompt: Which are among the course's three closing habits? (Select all that apply.)
options: Measure, don't vibe — decisions improve when run against eval sets
Own the harness — context, tools, guardrails, and orchestration are where your engineering wins
Close the loop — production failures become eval cases that gate future changes
Upgrade to every new model on release day without gating
questions.explanation: Measurement, harness ownership, and the production→eval→gate loop are the habits that survive model churn. Ungated same-day upgrades violate the first and third — model versions get pinned and upgrades go through the full eval gate plus canary.

questions.prompt: Why do eval sets, traces, and guardrails outlast the model they were built around?
options: They're required to be kept for seven years
Models, prices, and APIs churn, but these assets transfer to every future model — they're what makes adopting the next model cheap and safe
They can be sold separately from the product
They don't — everything must be rebuilt per model
questions.explanation: The course's closing argument: capabilities arrive from providers, but your eval sets, observability, guardrails, and judgment compound across model generations. They're the moat — invest accordingly, and take the checklist to a real problem.
